48	
49	# Reference Retriever
50	
51	You fetch the **original source files** — papers, books, online mathematical content — from the web and save them under the project's `references/` directory, then register each with a lightweight pointer file. You exist so the plan agent and blueprint-writers can read and quote the genuine source verbatim, rather than relying on a paraphrase (which drifts) or the dispatcher's memory (which fabricates).
52	
53	The single most important rule: **download the real file. Do not substitute a summary for it.** A paraphrase introduces exactly the errors (wrong lemma numbers, wrong tags, subtly mis-stated hypotheses) that this subagent exists to eliminate.
54	
55	## Your Job
56	
57	Your directive names one or more topics, mathematical questions, or specific sources (by arXiv ID, DOI, title + author, or URL). For each, you:
58	
59	1. Locate the source on the web (`WebSearch` + `WebFetch` for discovery).
60	2. **Download the original file(s)** with `curl`/`wget` via `Bash` into `references/`. Prefer machine-readable source (TeX/LaTeX) when it is openly available, AND keep the PDF too.
61	3. **Verify** the download is the real document, not an HTML paywall/error page saved under a `.pdf` name.
62	4. Write a lightweight **pointer file** `references/<slug>.md`: citation, local path(s), a contents/section map (table of contents), and caveats. **No paraphrased content.**
63	5. Register the new entry in `references/summary.md`.
64	6. Report back to the dispatcher with a one-line status + the report path.
65	
66	You do **not** hallucinate citations or content. If a source cannot be obtained through any legitimate open channel, you report that honestly. The dispatcher prefers an honest "not found" over a fabricated or paraphrased stand-in every time.
67

77	<one or two sentences naming the mathematical area + the specific question>

83	- arXiv: 1234.5678
84	- DOI: 10.xxxx/yyyy
85	- Title + author: "Some Paper" by Smith, year
86	- URL: https://...
87	- Search query: "<terms the dispatcher recommends>"
88	
89	(If no seeds are given, I search for the topic on arXiv + Google + Stacks Project + nLab as appropriate to the area.)
90	
91	## Out of scope
92	<sources or angles the dispatcher does NOT want — e.g. "no philosophy-of-math sources", "skip historical surveys, want technical content only">
93	
94	## Contents-map depth expected
95	- shallow: top-level table of contents only
96	- medium: section/chapter map with the relevant section(s) flagged + page numbers (default)
97	- deep: also map the specific theorems/lemmas the dispatcher named, with their exact location (section + page) inside the downloaded file — but still NO paraphrase of their content
98	```

100	If the directive omits "Contents-map depth expected", default to **medium**.

102	## What you do
103	
104	1. **Read your directive completely.**

106	2. **Locate the source.**
107	   - If seeds are given, resolve them first.
108	   - Otherwise, `WebSearch` with focused queries derived from the topic. Prefer:
109	     - arXiv for recent technical papers.
110	     - Stacks Project (https://stacks.math.columbia.edu) for algebraic geometry — its TeX source is open on GitHub.
111	     - nLab (https://ncatlab.org) for category-theoretic content.
112	     - Open-access textbooks and lecture notes when the topic is foundational.
113	
114	3. **Download the original file(s)** with `Bash`. Prefer machine-readable source and keep the PDF too:
115	
116	   - **arXiv**: fetch BOTH the LaTeX e-print and the PDF.
117	     ```bash
118	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.tar.gz https://arxiv.org/e-print/<id>
119	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.pdf     https://arxiv.org/pdf/<id>
120	     ```
121	     If the e-print is a single `.tex`, name it `references/<slug>.tex` instead. You may unpack the tarball (`tar xzf`) into `references/<slug>-src/` if it helps map the contents — but keep the original archive too.
122	
123	   - **Stacks Project**: fetch the relevant `.tex` chapter verbatim from GitHub raw, so lemma/tag statements are exact (this is the fix for the paraphrase/tag-typo failure mode):
124	     ```bash
125	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>-algebra.tex \
126	       https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stacks/stacks-project/master/algebra.tex
127	     ```
128	     Record the exact tags the dispatcher needs and confirm each tag's statement against the fetched `.tex`.
129	
130	   - **Journal / book with an open form**: download the open PDF (author homepage, institutional repository, numdam, Project Euclid open, publisher open-access, etc.).
131	
132	   - **General**:
133	     ```bash
134	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.pdf "<url>"
135	     ```
136	     `-f` makes curl fail on HTTP errors instead of silently saving an error page.
137	
138	4. **Verify the download is real**, not a paywall/login/error page wearing a `.pdf` extension:
139	   ```bash
140	   ls -l references/<slug>.pdf
141	   file references/<slug>.pdf      # expect "PDF document", not "HTML document"
142	   ```
143	   A near-empty file, an HTML MIME type, or a "log in to continue" body all mean the fetch FAILED — delete the bad file (`rm references/<slug>.pdf`) and treat that source as not obtained. **Never** rename or keep an unverified download.

146	   - **On success:** it points at the real downloaded file(s) and records the exact URL each was retrieved from. It does NOT reproduce the source's content.
147	   - **On failure** (nothing could be downloaded and verified): write the pointer anyway, recording the citation, every URL/channel you tried, and why each failed (paywall, 404, HTML-only, etc.). **Do NOT write the source's mathematical content into it** — a failure pointer carries the attempt log, never a paraphrase or recollection of what the source "probably says."

150	
151	7. **Verify the files are on disk** (`ls -l references/<slug>.*`) before reporting back.

153	## Acquiring paywalled / hard-to-find sources
154	
155	Many sources that look paywalled have a legitimate open copy. Before giving up, try, in order:
156	
157	- **arXiv** for the same title/authors — journal papers very often have an arXiv preprint with identical statements.
158	- **The author's personal / institutional homepage** — search `"<title>" <author> pdf` and `<author> publications`.
159	- **Open repositories**: numdam.org (French journals, seminars — this is where Grothendieck's FGA and Séminaire Bourbaki live), Project Euclid open content, institutional repositories, course pages.
160	- **`filetype:pdf` web searches**: `WebSearch` for `"<exact title>" filetype:pdf`. Some hits open a PDF directly — fetch and verify it.
161	- **Open TeX source**: Stacks Project (GitHub), many lecture notes and books publish LaTeX source openly.
162	
163	**Do NOT** use shadow-library / piracy sites (e.g. Sci-Hub, Library Genesis). Stick to legitimately open copies. If, after these steps, only a paywalled copy exists, fetch whatever open metadata you can (official TOC, abstract) into the pointer file's contents map, mark the source `PAYWALLED — no open copy located`, and say so plainly in your report. Do not paraphrase the body to compensate.

188

206	### Failure — nothing could be downloaded and verified
207	
208	Still write the pointer, but it is an **attempt log**, not content. A future planner reading it must immediately see that there is no local source to quote and what was already tried (so they don't burn an iteration repeating it).

210	```markdown

215

224	- <url 2> — <…>

231	```
232	
233	## `references/summary.md` registration
234	
235	Append one row to the `## File inventory` table. The template has three columns: `File`, `Description`, `How to read (confirmed working)`. Make the retrieval outcome visible in the index itself, so a planner skimming `summary.md` sees at a glance which sources actually have a local file AND which tool they should use to ingest each one:
236	
237	```markdown
238	| [`<slug>.md`](./<slug>.md) → `<slug>.pdf` / `<slug>.tex` | <one-line topic note> | `Read` (with `pages: "1-5"` if long); fallback `pdftotext <slug>.pdf -` |
239	| [`<slug>.md`](./<slug>.md) → ⚠ NOT RETRIEVED (<short reason>) | <topic note> | n/a — no local file |
240	```
241	
242	For the `How to read` column, record what *actually worked* during your verification step (you opened the file to verify it isn't an HTML-paywall stub — write down the command you used). `Read` for PDFs assumes ``poppler-utils`` is installed (``archon setup`` handles it); if `Read` errors with a missing-`pdftoppm` message, record the exact `pdftotext` (or other) fallback you used. The column is a living log — later agents who ingest the same file should overwrite the entry with a better entry if they find one.
243	
244	If `references/summary.md` doesn't exist, create it with the standard header + the inventory table before appending.
245	
246	## Rules
247	
248	### What you CAN do
249	- Use `WebSearch` and `WebFetch` to discover and inspect sources.
250	- Use `Bash` to download, unpack, and verify source files. **Use `curl` — it is present on every supported platform, so no extra install is needed.** `wget` is an optional fallback only if `curl` is somehow missing (`command -v curl || command -v wget`); never assume `wget` exists. `tar`, `file`, and `ls` are likewise standard.
251	- Write downloaded files and the pointer `.md` under `references/`.
252	- Append to `references/summary.md`.
253	
254	### What you MUST do
255	- **Download the real file and verify it.** A pointer file is only valid once its named source file exists on disk and `file` confirms it is the real document.
256	- **Prefer machine-readable source (TeX/LaTeX) when openly available, and keep the PDF too.** TeX gives exact statements; the PDF is the universal fallback.
257	- **Cite exact section / page numbers** in the contents map. A map that just says "the paper discusses X" is useless.
258	- **Quote nothing in the pointer file.** Planners quote from the downloaded source, not from you. Your contents map only tells them where to look.
259	
260	### What you MUST NOT do
261	- **Do NOT fabricate or paraphrase the source's content.** No "I believe the paper proves…", no reconstructed proofs in the `.md`. You fetched the file or you didn't.
262	- **Do NOT use piracy / shadow-library sites.** Legitimate open copies only.
263	- **Do NOT delete or rewrite existing `references/` entries.** Only add new ones. If an existing entry is wrong, report it in "Notes for Dispatcher" — do not modify it.
264	- **Do NOT write outside `references/`.**
265	- **Do NOT spawn child subagents.** You are a leaf.
266	
267	## Report format
268	
269	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/reference-retriever-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).

275	<slug>
276

279	<NOT_FOUND: no legitimate open copy of any source could be downloaded.
280	PARTIAL: some sources/formats obtained, others not (e.g. PDF but no TeX, or paywalled).
281	COMPLETE: all directive items downloaded and verified.>
282	
283	## Sources fetched
284	
285	For each: title, URL(s) used, formats downloaded, verification result, pointer file written.
286	
287	- "Some Paper" — https://arxiv.org/abs/1234.5678 — downloaded `references/some-paper.pdf` (PDF, verified) + `references/some-paper.tar.gz` (TeX source, verified) — pointer `references/some-paper.md`.
288	- "Other Book Ch. 5" — paywalled, no open copy located — TOC-only pointer written, NO source file.
289	
290	## Index updates
291	- `references/summary.md` — appended <N> entries: `some-paper`, ...
292	
293	## Notes for Dispatcher
294	<- any source the directive named that turned out to be wrong / mislocated, with the corrected pointer
295	- any related source you noticed that the dispatcher might want next
296	- any pre-existing references/ entry you noticed was wrong or stale (DO NOT modify — report only)
297	- any source you could only obtain paywalled, so the dispatcher can decide whether to find another route>
298	```

303	
304	- One line: `<slug>: <status> — <N> sources downloaded (<formats>), <M> pointer files written`
305	- The path to your full report.

2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.

The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

2339	(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})_x
2340	=
2341	\mathcal{F}_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}} \mathcal{G}_x
2342	$$
2343	functorial in $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$.
2344	\end{lemma}
2345	
2346	\begin{proof}
2347	Omitted.
2348	\end{proof}
2349	
2350	\begin{lemma}
2351	\label{lemma-tensor-product-sheafification}
2352	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2353	Let $\mathcal{F}'$, $\mathcal{G}'$ be presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
2354	with sheafifications $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$. Then
2355	$\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} =
2356	(\mathcal{F}' \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}')^\#$.
2357	\end{lemma}
2358	
2359	\begin{proof}
2360	Omitted.
2361	\end{proof}
2362	
2363	
2364	\begin{lemma}
2365	\label{lemma-tensor-product-exact}
2366	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2367	Let $\mathcal{G}$ be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
2368	If
2369	$\mathcal{F}_1
2370	\to \mathcal{F}_2
2371	\to \mathcal{F}_3
2372	\to 0$
2373	is an exact sequence of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules then
2374	the induced sequence
2375	$$
2376	\mathcal{F}_1 \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
2377	\mathcal{F}_2 \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
2378	\mathcal{F}_3 \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
2379	0
2380	$$

4038	\section{Invertible modules}
4039	\label{section-invertible}
4040	
4041	\noindent
4042	Similarly to the case of modules over rings
4043	(More on Algebra, Section \ref{more-algebra-section-picard})
4044	we have the following definition.
4045	
4046	\begin{definition}
4047	\label{definition-invertible}
4048	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. An
4049	{\it invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module} is a sheaf
4050	of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{L}$ such that
4051	the functor
4052	$$
4053	\textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \longrightarrow \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X),\quad
4054	\mathcal{F} \longmapsto \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}
4055	$$
4056	is an equivalence of categories. We say that $\mathcal{L}$ is
4057	{\it trivial} if it is isomorphic as an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
4058	to $\mathcal{O}_X$.
4059	\end{definition}
4060	
4061	\noindent
4062	Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}
4063	below explains the relationship with locally free modules
4064	of rank $1$.
4065	
4066	\begin{lemma}
4067	\label{lemma-invertible}
4068	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Let $\mathcal{L}$
4069	be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Equivalent are
4070	\begin{enumerate}
4071	\item $\mathcal{L}$ is invertible, and
4072	\item there exists an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{N}$
4073	such that
4074	$\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X$.
4075	\end{enumerate}
4076	In this case $\mathcal{L}$ is locally a direct summand of a finite free
4077	$\mathcal{O}_X$-module and the module $\mathcal{N}$ in (2) is isomorphic to
4078	$\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$.
4079	\end{lemma}
4080	
4081	\begin{proof}
4082	Assume (1). Then the functor $- \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
4083	is essentially surjective, hence there exists an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
4084	$\mathcal{N}$ as in (2). If (2) holds, then the functor
4085	$- \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N}$ is a quasi-inverse
4086	to the functor $- \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$ and
4087	we see that (1) holds.
4088	
4089	\medskip\noindent
4090	Assume (1) and (2) hold. Denote
4091	$\psi : \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \to \mathcal{O}_X$
4092	the given isomorphism. Let $x \in X$. Choose an open neighbourhood
4093	$U$ an integer $n \geq 1$ and sections $s_i \in \mathcal{L}(U)$,
4094	$t_i \in \mathcal{N}(U)$ such that $\psi(\sum s_i \otimes t_i) = 1$.
4095	Consider the isomorphisms
4096	$$
4097	\mathcal{L}|_U \to
4098	\mathcal{L}|_U \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_U}
4099	\mathcal{L}|_U \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_U} \mathcal{N}|_U \to \mathcal{L}|_U
4100	$$
4101	where the first arrow sends $s$ to $\sum s_i \otimes s \otimes t_i$
4102	and the second arrow sends $s \otimes s' \otimes t$ to $\psi(s' \otimes t)s$.
4103	We conclude that $s \mapsto \sum \psi(s \otimes t_i)s_i$ is
4104	an automorphism of $\mathcal{L}|_U$. This automorphism factors as
4105	$$
4106	\mathcal{L}|_U \to \mathcal{O}_U^{\oplus n} \to \mathcal{L}|_U
4107	$$
4108	where the first arrow is given by
4109	$s \mapsto (\psi(s \otimes t_1), \ldots, \psi(s \otimes t_n))$
4110	and the second arrow by $(a_1, \ldots, a_n) \mapsto \sum a_i s_i$.
4111	In this way we conclude that $\mathcal{L}|_U$ is a direct summand
4112	of a finite free $\mathcal{O}_U$-module.
4113	
4114	\medskip\noindent
4115	Assume (1) and (2) hold. Consider the evaluation map
4116	$$
4117	\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
4118	\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)
4119	\longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_X
4120	$$
4121	To finish the proof of the lemma
4122	we will show this is an isomorphism by checking it induces
4123	isomorphisms on stalks. Let $x \in X$.
4124	Since we know (by the previous paragraph)
4125	that $\mathcal{L}$ is a finitely presented
4126	$\mathcal{O}_X$-module
4127	we can use Lemma \ref{lemma-stalk-internal-hom}
4128	to see that it suffices to show that
4129	$$
4130	\mathcal{L}_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}}
4131	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}}(\mathcal{L}_x, \mathcal{O}_{X, x})
4132	\longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{X, x}
4133	$$
4134	is an isomorphism. Since
4135	$\mathcal{L}_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}} \mathcal{N}_x =
4136	(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N})_x =
4137	\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ (Lemma \ref{lemma-stalk-tensor-product})
4138	the desired result follows from
4139	More on Algebra, Lemma \ref{more-algebra-lemma-invertible}.
4140	\end{proof}
4141	
4142	\begin{lemma}
4143	\label{lemma-pullback-invertible}
4144	Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a
4145	morphism of ringed spaces. The pullback $f^*\mathcal{L}$ of an
4146	invertible $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module is invertible.
4147	\end{lemma}
4148	
4149	\begin{proof}
4150	By Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}
4151	there exists an $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module $\mathcal{N}$ such that
4152	$\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_Y} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_Y$.
4153	Pulling back we get
4154	$f^*\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} f^*\mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X$
4155	by Lemma \ref{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}.
4156	Thus $f^*\mathcal{L}$ is invertible by Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}.
4157	\end{proof}
4158	
4159	\begin{lemma}
4160	\label{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}
4161	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
4162	$\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
4163	If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
4164	the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case).
4165	\end{lemma}
4166	
4167	\begin{proof}
4168	The parenthetical statement follows by considering a one point
4169	space $X$ with sheaf of rings $\mathcal{O}_X$ given by a ring $R$.
4170	Then invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules correspond to invertible
4171	$R$-modules, hence as soon as $\Pic(R)$ is not the trivial group,
4172	then we get an example.
4173	
4174	\medskip\noindent
4175	Assume $\mathcal{L}$ is locally free of rank $1$ and consider the
4176	evaluation map
4177	$$
4178	\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
4179	\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)
4180	\longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_X
4181	$$
4182	Looking over an open covering trivialization $\mathcal{L}$, we see
4183	that this map is an isomorphism. Hence $\mathcal{L}$ is invertible
4184	by Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}.
4185	
4186	\medskip\noindent
4187	Assume all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings and $\mathcal{L}$
4188	invertible. In the proof of Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}
4189	we have seen that $\mathcal{L}_x$ is an invertible
4190	$\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$-module for all $x \in X$. Since
4191	$\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ is local, we see that
4192	$\mathcal{L}_x \cong \mathcal{O}_{X, x}$
4193	(More on Algebra, Section \ref{more-algebra-section-picard}).
4194	Since $\mathcal{L}$ is of finite presentation by
4195	Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible} we conclude that $\mathcal{L}$
4196	is locally free of rank $1$ by
4197	Lemma \ref{lemma-finite-presentation-stalk-free}.
4198	\end{proof}
4199	
4200	\begin{lemma}
4201	\label{lemma-constructions-invertible}
4202	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
4203	\begin{enumerate}
4204	\item If $\mathcal{L}$, $\mathcal{N}$ are invertible
4205	$\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, then so is
4206	$\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N}$.
4207	\item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
4208	$\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
4209	$\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
4210	\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
4211	is an isomorphism.
4212	\end{enumerate}
4213	\end{lemma}
4214	
4215	\begin{proof}
4216	Part (1) is clear from the definition and part (2) follows from
4217	Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible} and its proof.
4218	\end{proof}
4219	
4220	\begin{definition}
4221	\label{definition-powers}
4222	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Given an invertible sheaf
4223	$\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ and $n \in \mathbf{Z}$ we define the
4224	$n$th {\it tensor power} $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ of $\mathcal{L}$
4225	as the image of $\mathcal{O}_X$ under applying the equivalence
4226	$\mathcal{F} \mapsto\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
4227	exactly $n$ times.
4228	\end{definition}
4229	
4230	\noindent
4231	This makes sense also for negative $n$ as we've defined an invertible
4232	$\mathcal{O}_X$-module as one for which tensoring is an equivalence.
4233	More explicitly, we have
4234	$$
4235	\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} =
4236	\left\{
4237	\begin{matrix}
4238	\mathcal{O}_X & \text{if} & n = 0 \\
4239	\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) & \text{if} & n = -1\\
4240	\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \ldots \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}
4241	& \text{if} & n > 0 \\
4242	\mathcal{L}^{\otimes -1} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \ldots
4243	\otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes -1}
4244	& \text{if} & n < -1
4245	\end{matrix}
4246	\right.
4247	$$
4248	see Lemma \ref{lemma-constructions-invertible}.
4249	With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
4250	$\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
4251	\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
4252	\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
4253	satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
4254	(formulation omitted).
4255	
4256	\medskip\noindent
4257	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. We can define a $\mathbf{Z}$-graded
4258	ring structure on $\bigoplus \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ by mapping
4259	$s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
4260	$t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the
4261	section corresponding to $s \otimes t$ in
4262	$\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m})$.
4263	We omit the verification that this defines a commutative
4264	and associative ring with $1$. However, by our conventions in
4265	Algebra, Section \ref{algebra-section-graded}
4266	a graded ring has no nonzero elements in negative degrees.
4267	This leads to the following definition.
4268	
4269	\begin{definition}
4270	\label{definition-gamma-star}
4271	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
4272	Given an invertible sheaf $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ we define
4273	the {\it associated graded ring} to be
4274	$$
4275	\Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L})
4276	=
4277	\bigoplus\nolimits_{n \geq 0} \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})
4278	$$
4279	Given a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ we set
4280	$$
4281	\Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L}, \mathcal{F})
4282	=
4283	\bigoplus\nolimits_{n \in \mathbf{Z}} \Gamma(X,
4284	\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})
4285	$$
4286	which we think of as a graded $\Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L})$-module.
4287	\end{definition}
4288	
4289	\noindent
4290	We often write simply $\Gamma_*(\mathcal{L})$ and $\Gamma_*(\mathcal{F})$
4291	(although this is ambiguous if $\mathcal{F}$ is invertible).
4292	The multiplication of $\Gamma_*(\mathcal{L})$ on
4293	$\Gamma_*(\mathcal{F})$ is defined using the isomorphisms
4294	above. If $\gamma : \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G}$ is a $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
4295	map, then we get an $\Gamma_*(\mathcal{L})$-module homomorphism
4296	$\gamma : \Gamma_*(\mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma_*(\mathcal{G})$.
4297	If $\alpha : \mathcal{L} \to \mathcal{N}$ is an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
4298	map between invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, then we obtain
4299	a graded ring homomorphism $\Gamma_*(\mathcal{L}) \to \Gamma_*(\mathcal{N})$.
4300	If $f : (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y) \to (X, \mathcal{O}_X)$
4301	is a morphism of ringed spaces and if $\mathcal{L}$ is invertible
4302	on $X$, then we get an invertible sheaf $f^*\mathcal{L}$ on $Y$
4303	(Lemma \ref{lemma-pullback-invertible})
4304	and an induced homomorphism of graded rings
4305	$$
4306	f^* :
4307	\Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L})
4308	\longrightarrow
4309	\Gamma_*(Y, f^*\mathcal{L})
4310	$$
4311	Furthermore, there are some compatibilities between the constructions
4312	above whose statements we omit.
4313	
4314	\begin{lemma}
4315	\label{lemma-pic-set}
4316	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
4317	There exists a set of invertible modules $\{\mathcal{L}_i\}_{i \in I}$
4318	such that each invertible module on $X$ is isomorphic to exactly
4319	one of the $\mathcal{L}_i$.
4320	\end{lemma}
4321	
4322	\begin{proof}
4323	Recall that any invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module is locally
4324	a direct summand of a finite free $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, see
4325	Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}.
4326	For each open covering $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{j \in J} U_j$
4327	and map $r : J \to \mathbf{N}$ consider the sheaves of
4328	$\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ such that
4329	$\mathcal{F}_j = \mathcal{F}|_{U_j}$ is a direct summand of
4330	$\mathcal{O}_{U_j}^{\oplus r(j)}$.
4331	The collection of isomorphism classes of $\mathcal{F}_j$ is a set, because
4332	$\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U^{\oplus r}, \mathcal{O}_U^{\oplus r})$
4333	is a set. The sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ is gotten by glueing $\mathcal{F}_j$,
4334	see Sheaves, Section
4335	\ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves}. Note that the collection of
4336	all glueing data forms a set. The collection of all coverings
4337	$\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{j \in J} U_i$ where $J \to \mathcal{P}(X)$,
4338	$j \mapsto U_j$ is injective forms a set as well. For each covering
4339	there is a set of maps $r : J \to \mathbf{N}$. Hence the collection
4340	of all $\mathcal{F}$ forms a set.
4341	\end{proof}
4342	
4343	\noindent
4344	This lemma says roughly speaking that the collection of
4345	isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves forms a set.
4346	Lemma \ref{lemma-constructions-invertible} says that
4347	tensor product defines the structure of an abelian group
4348	on this set.
4349	
4350	\begin{definition}
4351	\label{definition-pic}
4352	Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
4353	The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
4354	abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
4355	invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
4356	corresponding to tensor product.
4357	\end{definition}

# Section 17.25: Invertible Modules

**Tag/Label:** 01CR

**Location:** Chapter 17 (Sheaves of Modules) in The Stacks Project

**Main Statement:**

This section develops the theory of invertible modules on ringed spaces. The key definition states that an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module is "a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{L}$ such that the functor...tensoring with $\mathcal{L}$ is an equivalence of categories."

The section establishes several fundamental results:

1. **Characterization:** An invertible module is equivalent to one having an inverse module $\mathcal{N}$ where their tensor product yields the structure sheaf.

2. **Local Structure:** Invertible modules are locally direct summands of finite free modules.

3. **Relationship to Locally Free Modules:** Any rank-1 locally free module is invertible; the converse holds when all stalks are local rings.

4. **Tensor Operations:** The tensor product and dual of invertible modules remain invertible, forming an abelian group structure.

5. **Picard Group:** The collection of isomorphism classes of invertible modules forms the Picard group, with tensor product as the group operation.
